OIIX A4458/24.explanation: This is an important message for pilots flying between Tehran and Kabul airspaces. If you're flying from Kabul into Tehran, you must contact Tehran air traffic control at least 5 minutes before crossing the border, using specific radio frequencies for your route. However, if you're flying from Tehran into Kabul, you do so at your own risk. Air traffic services will only be provided up to the border with Kabul airspace, meaning you're on your own once you enter Kabul's airspace.

OIIX A4459/24.explanation: This is a warning from Iran to its airlines about flying in, out of, or over Afghanistan. It says that the airspace over Afghanistan is dangerous because there aren't proper air traffic control services, there's a risk from militant groups, and it's hard to deal with emergencies. Iranian airlines are told to be very careful and consider these serious risks when planning any flights in that area.

OIIX A4460/24.explanation: This is an official warning from Iran's aviation authority to Iranian airlines and pilots. It tells them there's a serious security risk when flying over certain areas in and around Ukraine, Rostov (Russia), and Moldova. The danger comes from increased military activity, anti-aircraft weapons, and the potential for mistakes or misunderstandings due to the ongoing conflict in Ukraine. Pilots are advised to carefully consider these risks when planning flights in these regions.

OIIX A4461/24.explanation: This is an urgent safety warning for all aircraft operators. They are strongly advised not to fly into the airspace over Sudan (known as Khartoum FIR) because it is currently very dangerous. There's a high risk from anti-aircraft weapons and intense military activity. This warning is issued by Iran's aviation authority and will be in effect from November 2024 until February 2025. Pilots should contact the Iran Civil Aviation Authority for more details and check the provided ICAO website for alternative flight routes.

OIIX A0863/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots about specific, mandatory flight paths that planes must use when flying to Tehran's Imam Khomeini International Airport (OIMM) from three other domestic airports (OISS, OIBB, and OIYY). It's part of a traffic management plan to ensure planes follow designated routes.

OIIX A0893/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots about several 'highways in the sky' (airways) in Iran that will be temporarily closed. These specific routes will be unavailable for aircraft to fly on from May 5, 2026, until August 3, 2026. Pilots will need to plan alternative routes during this time.

OIIX A0912/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in Iran. A specific air route, called Airway A647, between two points named NAZAR and RAGET, has been changed. Previously, aircraft might have only been able to fly in one direction on this segment, but now they can fly in both directions. This is a change to the air traffic rules for that specific 'highway in the sky'.

OIIX A0957/26.explanation: A vital navigation beacon, called VOR ANK, which helps aircraft find their way, is currently not working. It operates on frequency 112.700 MHz and will be out of service from May 12, 2026, until August 10, 2026. Once repaired, it will need to be tested by an aircraft in flight to ensure it's working correctly before it can be used again.

OIIX A0973/26.explanation: This is an update to the 'Traffic Orientation Scheme' for domestic flights within the Tehran airspace. It changes specific flight paths (routes for taking off and landing) between various airports in Iran. Think of it as updating the 'road map' for planes flying between these cities.

OIIX A0974/26.explanation: This is an official aviation notice announcing changes to the specific flight paths (routes) that aircraft must follow when flying into or out of major airports in Iran (Tehran, Shiraz, Mashhad) and when traveling to or from neighboring airspaces like Muscat (Oman) and Baghdad (Iraq). It updates the 'Traffic Orientation Scheme' for international arrivals and departures, essentially changing the designated 'roads in the sky' for these regions.

OIIX A1056/26.explanation: This is a permanent update for pilots flying in Iran. A specific area called 'OIR42 Uromiyeh' that was previously restricted for flights has now been completely removed. This means aircraft can now fly through this area without any special permissions or limitations. Pilots should update their navigation charts accordingly.

OIIX A1006/26.explanation: An area in Iran, identified as OID84, will be off-limits for all aircraft, including both commercial and private planes, from May 28 to July 22, 2026. This restriction will be in effect every day during specific hours (between 03:30 AM and 07:30 PM UTC) and will cover altitudes from the ground up to 13,000 feet above sea level. Pilots must refer to official aviation documents for more information about this newly activated restricted zone.

OIIX A1008/26.explanation: An area in Iran (called OID126) will be restricted for aircraft from May 28, 2026, to July 22, 2026. During this period, the restriction will be active daily between 03:30 AM and 04:30 PM UTC. No aircraft will be allowed to fly in this area from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX B0248/26.explanation: Attention all aircraft: There will be gun firing exercises taking place in a specific area. This will happen daily from May 28, 2026, to July 22, 2026, between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM local time. The danger zone extends from the ground up to 15,000 feet above sea level. Aircraft should avoid this area during these times.

OIIX A1053/26.explanation: This is an aviation notice about a specific area called OID122 in the Tehran region. This area will be restricted, meaning aircraft are not allowed to fly there, from the ground up to 16,000 feet. This restriction will be active daily from 02:30 AM to 08:30 PM UTC, starting on May 30, 2026, and ending on July 22, 2026. Pilots need to be aware of this restricted zone.

OIIX A1054/26.explanation: Attention pilots! A specific area within the Tehran airspace, identified as OID123, is now restricted. This means aircraft are not allowed to fly there. This restriction is active daily from May 30th to July 22nd, between 02:30 AM and 08:30 PM UTC. The restricted zone extends from the ground up to 18,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX A1055/26.explanation: An area called OID128 in the Tehran region will be restricted for aircraft. This means planes cannot fly there. It will be active daily from May 30, 2026, to July 22, 2026, between 02:30 AM and 04:30 PM UTC, from the ground up to 15,000 feet. Pilots must check their routes to avoid this area.

OIIX B0251/26.explanation: This is a warning for pilots that gun firing will be taking place in a specific area within Iran. The firing will occur daily between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM UTC, from May 30 to July 22, 2026. The affected area extends from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level. Aircraft should be aware of this activity.

OIIX B0252/26.explanation: Attention pilots! There will be gun firing in a specific area within Iran's airspace. This activity will take place daily from May 30, 2026, until July 22, 2026, between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM UTC each day. The affected airspace is from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level. Aircraft should avoid this area during these times.

OIIX A1058/26.explanation: Attention all pilots and air travelers! From May 30th to July 22nd, daily between 3:30 AM and 8:30 PM UTC, there will be gun firing exercises in a specific area within Iran. This activity will take place from the ground up to 20,000 feet in the air. Aircraft should be aware of this military activity and avoid the designated area during these times for safety.

OIIX B0255/26.explanation: Attention pilots and air travelers! There will be gun firing exercises taking place in a specific area near Mashhad, Iran. These exercises will occur daily between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM local time, starting from May 30, 2026, and continuing until July 22, 2026. The affected airspace is from the ground up to 11,000 feet above sea level. Please be aware of this activity if flying in the region.

OIIX B0254/26.explanation: An area of airspace called OID124 is now restricted for aircraft. This means planes cannot fly there without special permission. It will be active every day from May 30th to July 22nd, 2026, specifically between 4:30 AM and 8:30 PM Universal Coordinated Time (UTC). The restricted zone starts from the ground and goes up to 13,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should check their aviation charts for details about this area.

OIIX A1102/26.explanation: This is a warning from Iran's aviation authority about a dangerous situation over a large area of international waters, from sea level up to 10,000 feet, between June 2 and July 2, 2026. Due to increased military and naval activities, electronic warfare, and air defense readiness, there's a high risk of civil aircraft being mistaken for something else, sudden flight path changes, GPS and communication problems, more military aircraft intercepts, and quick closures of air routes. All aircraft operators, especially Iranian ones, are advised to be extremely careful, assess risks before flying, constantly check for updates, have backup plans for diversions or communication failures, and immediately follow air traffic control instructions.

OIIX A1112/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots and air traffic controllers in the Tehran region of Iran. It announces a temporary update to the established flight paths for international planes flying into and out of the area. Specifically, it changes certain routes (identified by names like PAXAT, ILM, UKSIS, KIXOB, and numbers like B411, G202, L223, W3) that connect the airspace over Bahrain with the airspace over Shiraz. This change is part of a larger traffic plan and will be in effect from June 3, 2026, to August 3, 2026. It's essentially an update to the 'highways in the sky' for aircraft.

OIIX A1113/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in Iran's airspace. It announces temporary changes to the specific routes (like designated highways in the sky) that domestic flights must use when traveling between Shiraz and Ahwaz airports. These changes are effective from early June to early August 2026 and are part of a larger traffic management plan.

OIIX A1183/26.explanation: This message confirms that the air traffic control hand-off points between Tehran's airspace and the airspaces of Turkey (Ankara), Armenia (Yerevan), and Azerbaijan (Nakhchivan and Baku) are available for international flights that are flying into or out of Tehran's region. Essentially, it means international flights can be properly transferred between these countries' air traffic controllers.

OIIX A1185/26.explanation: This NOTAM means that all previous rules for planes flying over Iran (specifically the OIIX region) are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes have been put in place for planes flying above 28,500 feet during what's called a 'recovery period.' For example, planes flying from Oman to Pakistan must use a specific path (DENDA R462 METBI), and planes flying the other way must use another specific path (METBI R462 DENDA). This indicates an unusual situation requiring changes to how air traffic flows.

OIIX A1186/26.explanation: This notice means that for a specific period, all previous rules for planes flying high above 28,500 feet over Tehran's airspace (Iran) are temporarily stopped. New, specific flight paths have been set up for these high-altitude flights, especially for planes traveling between Karachi (Pakistan) and Baku (Azerbaijan) airspaces. This change is happening during a 'recovery period,' suggesting it's a response to a previous event or disruption.

OIIX A1187/26.explanation: This is an alert from Iran's air traffic control. It means that the usual rules for planes flying through Iran's airspace (specifically above 28,500 feet) are temporarily changed. All old rules for these flights are stopped. New, specific routes are now mandatory for planes flying between Pakistan and India (Kolkata) through this airspace. This change is in effect during what they call a 'recovery period,' suggesting there was a previous issue that required these new flight paths to be put in place.

OIIX A1188/26.explanation: This notice means that the usual rules for planes flying over a certain area (specifically above 28,500 feet) are temporarily stopped. New, specific flight paths are being put in place for planes traveling between two air traffic control regions (OAKX and UTAA) for a few weeks, during what's called a 'recovery period.' This means pilots must follow these new, designated routes instead of the old ones.

OIIX A1189/26.explanation: Iran's airspace has new temporary rules for planes flying over it, specifically above 28,500 feet. All previous rules for these overflights are suspended. New, specific routes must be followed for planes traveling between Afghanistan and Azerbaijan through Iranian airspace during what's called a 'recovery period'. This change is in effect from June 8th to June 29th, 2026.

OIIX A1190/26.explanation: This is an important update for airplanes flying over Iran. All previous rules for flying over Iran at high altitudes (above 28,500 feet) are temporarily stopped. New specific routes must now be used for these high-altitude flights, especially for those going between Muscat (Oman) and Azerbaijan, and also for flights landing in the UAE. This change is in effect from June 8th to June 29th, 2026, during what is called a 'recovery period'.

OIIX A1191/26.explanation: This message from Iran's air traffic control states that for a specific period (June 8-29, 2026), all previous flight paths for planes flying over Iran at high altitudes (above 28,500 feet) are temporarily suspended. New, specific routes are now mandatory for planes flying between Oman and Turkmenistan. This change is happening during a 'recovery period,' suggesting there was a previous event that disrupted normal air travel.

OIIX A1192/26.explanation: This is an urgent message from Iran's air traffic control. It means that all previous rules for planes flying over Iran are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes must now be followed by planes flying above 28,500 feet, especially for flights between Oman and Pakistan, and for planes landing in the UAE. These changes are in effect during a 'recovery period,' suggesting there was a recent event or disruption that required these new flight paths.

OIIX A1306/26.explanation: Tehran's airspace is now open again for planes flying through, but with several important rules. Flights above 28,500 feet will have radar monitoring where available. If radar isn't available, planes flying in the same direction at the same altitude over specific points must be kept at least 50 nautical miles apart. Air traffic control might also increase the distance between planes if needed. All planes must contact Tehran Air Traffic Control 5 minutes before entering its airspace and contact the next control center 5 minutes before leaving. Every flight must have a working collision avoidance system (ACAS) and use a specific transponder code (or 2000 if none is assigned) until told otherwise.

OIIX A1311/26.explanation: From June 22 to September 19, 2026, all aircraft flying in a specific part of the Tehran airspace must have advanced tracking equipment called a Mode S transponder with ADS-B capability, or dedicated ADS-B Out. This rule applies to all flights, except for emergency medical flights. This is to ensure better tracking and identification of aircraft in that area.

OIIX A1316/26.explanation: This is a warning for all flights in the Persian Gulf and parts of the Oman Sea (west of 59 degrees East longitude), within the airspace managed by Tehran. It states that a nearby conflict zone might expand into these international waters due to security concerns. Pilots should be very careful when flying in this area.

OIIX B0298/26.explanation: Most private and recreational flights (General Aviation and VFR flights) are stopped in Iran until September 19, 2026. However, some flights are still allowed: training flights at specific local airports, emergency flights (like hospital, search and rescue), government and military flights, and other VFR flights if you get special permission. Also, large aircraft flights (IFR) that briefly switch to VFR near airports without navigation aids are not affected.

OIIX A1334/26.explanation: An important air route, called Airway N39, within Iran's airspace (Tehran FIR) is temporarily closed between two specific points (DEMBA and OBRIX). This affects planes flying through this area. Pilots must use alternative routes provided, depending on their direction of travel (westbound or eastbound), and maintain specific minimum altitudes. This closure is in effect from June 23 to September 21, 2026.

OIIX A1335/26.explanation: An important air route, G208/L125, in the Tehran flight region is temporarily closed from June 23 to September 21, 2026. Planes flying through this area that would normally use this route must now take a different, specified path. However, a specific point on the route called RADAL remains open for planes arriving at Tehran's Imam Khomeini, Mehrabad, and Payam airports.

OIIX A1417/26.explanation: The radar system in Tabriz, Iran, is currently out of order. This means air traffic controllers will not be able to see aircraft on their radar screens for planes flying long distances (en-route) in certain parts of the airspace, specifically in Sector 1. This situation is expected to last from June 30, 2026, until September 22, 2026. Pilots flying in this region should be aware that radar services may not be available.

OIIX A1418/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots and airlines about a change in how aircraft can navigate on all air routes in Iran. Starting from June 30, 2026, all these routes will be available for aircraft that use a specific navigation method called 'RNAV 5' (Area Navigation 5), which allows for more flexible routes. This change applies from the minimum safe altitude upwards and is a technical update for flight planning.

OIIX A1419/26.explanation: A radar system in the Ahwaz region of Iran is currently broken. This means air traffic controllers might not be able to see planes on their radar screens in some parts of the airspace, specifically in certain flight control sectors. Flights in this area might experience changes or delays because controllers will have to use other methods to guide aircraft. This situation is expected to last from late June to late September 2026.

OIIX A1421/26.explanation: The radar system in Shiraz, Iran, is broken and will not be working from June 30 to September 22, 2026. This means air traffic controllers will not be able to see planes on radar in certain parts of the airspace, specifically in two control sectors (Sector 3 and Sector 4). Pilots flying through these areas will need to rely on other methods for navigation and communication.

OIIX A1423/26.explanation: A radar system in Iran is currently broken and out of service. This means that air traffic controllers will not be able to see aircraft on their radar screens in certain areas, specifically in parts of Air Traffic Control Sector 4. This will affect how planes are guided while flying long distances (en-route) in that region.

OIIX A1426/26.explanation: A radar system in Esfahan, Iran, is currently broken and will be out of service until late September 2026. This means air traffic controllers might not have radar information for planes flying through certain parts of the airspace, specifically in sectors 2, 3, 6 West, and 7. Pilots flying in these areas might not receive radar services.

OIIX A1428/26.explanation: The main radar system at Tehran (Mehrabad) airport, which helps air traffic controllers track planes flying between cities, is currently broken. Because of this, controllers might not be able to see aircraft on their radar screens in certain parts of the airspace (specifically sectors 1, 2, and 7). This situation is expected to last for several months, from June 30th to September 22nd, 2026. Pilots flying in this area need to be aware that radar services may not be available.

OIIX A1427/26.explanation: A radar system at Kish airport in Iran is broken and will not be working until September 22, 2026. This means air traffic controllers might not have radar information for planes flying in certain areas, especially near Kish and in two specific air traffic control sectors (Sector 4 and Sector 5). Pilots flying in these areas might not receive full radar services.

OIIX A1429/26.explanation: The main radar system at Tehran's Imam Khomeini Airport, which helps air traffic controllers track planes flying long distances (en-route), is currently broken. This means that in certain parts of the airspace controlled by Tehran, air traffic controllers might not have radar information to see planes. This could affect how flights are managed in those specific areas until September 22, 2026.

OIIX A1430/26.explanation: A major radar system in Iran, called Kushk MSSR, is broken and will be out of service for several months, from late June to late September 2026. This means air traffic controllers won't be able to use radar to track planes in certain parts of Iran's airspace (specifically, en-route sectors 1, 2, and 7). Pilots flying through these areas will not receive normal radar services, which could affect how air traffic is managed and how flights are routed.

OIIX A1455/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in the Tehran region. It provides new direct and mobile phone numbers for Air Traffic Control (ACC) that pilots might need to contact.

OIIX B0349/26.explanation: This is an aviation notice about a new telephone number for Tehran Air Traffic Control. The new number is 00982149252331. This change is effective from July 3, 2026, at 07:47 UTC until October 2, 2026, at 07:30 UTC.

OIIX A1456/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots and airlines flying in or through Iran. It provides new phone numbers for the office that processes flight plans (IFPS office).

OIIX A1457/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in the Tehran area. It provides new phone numbers for the office that handles flight permissions. These numbers will be active from July 3rd to October 2nd, 2026.

OIIX A1202/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots and airlines. It means that on July 9, 2026, there will be no new updates or changes to the standard aviation information documents (like maps and procedures) that pilots use. Usually, updates are released on specific dates, but for this particular date, there won't be any.

OIIX A1522/26.explanation: A radar system in Mashhad, Iran, is broken and will not be working from July 12, 2026, until September 12, 2026. This means air traffic controllers won't have radar information for planes flying to, from, or over Mashhad, and some radar services in a specific air traffic control area might also be unavailable during this time.

OIIX B0358/26.explanation: This is an administrative notice for pilots and aviation staff in Iranian airspace. It's like a master list or table of contents, telling them which other important aviation notices (NOTAMs), publications, and circulars are currently active and should be reviewed. It also mentions specific dates when new updates won't be published. Essentially, it helps ensure everyone has the most up-to-date information for safe operations.

OIIX A1537/26.explanation: This is an administrative notice for pilots, acting like a checklist. It lists all the other important aviation notices (NOTAMs) that are currently active and should be reviewed. It also provides updates on official aviation documents, such as Aeronautical Information Publications (AIPs), which contain crucial information for flying. Finally, it informs pilots that no new major updates to these documents will be published on specific dates in July, August, and September 2026. Essentially, it's a guide to help pilots ensure they have all the latest and necessary information for safe flights in the Tehran Flight Information Region.

OIIX A1517/26.explanation: There will be gun firing in a specific area from July 14th to July 20th, 2026. This will happen daily between 03:30 AM and 07:30 PM local time. The affected area extends from the ground up to 20,000 feet above sea level. Aircraft should avoid this area during these times.

OIIX B0356/26.explanation: This is an aviation warning about gun firing activities in a specific area within Iran. The activities will take place daily from July 14 to July 20, 2026, between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM UTC. The affected area extends from the ground up to 13,000 feet above sea level.

KICZ A0016/20.explanation: This is a security warning from the U.S. government (FAA) for American airlines, pilots, and aircraft flying over the Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. Due to ongoing military activity and political tensions in the region, they should be very careful. They are advised to avoid routes close to Iranian airspace if possible, be aware of potential GPS and communication problems, and report any safety or security issues to the FAA. A previous ban on flying in Iranian airspace is still active.

KICZ A0011/24.explanation: The United States has issued a warning that bans certain flights from flying in the airspace over Tehran, Iran. This ban started on October 4, 2024, and will remain in effect until October 31, 2027. This is a special rule from the U.S. aviation authority, and pilots and airlines must read the full details of this regulation.

OIIX A1518/26.explanation: An area in the Tehran region, identified as OID121, will be restricted for aircraft from July 14th to July 20th, 2026. During these dates, no aircraft are allowed to fly in this area from the ground up to 30,000 feet, daily between 03:30 AM and 07:30 PM UTC. This is a warning for all pilots.

OIIX A1520/26.explanation: A specific danger zone in Iranian airspace, called OID127, will be active on July 26th, 2026, from 3:30 AM to 7:30 PM UTC. This area extends from the ground up to 12,000 feet above sea level. Pilots flying in this region should be aware of this activated danger area.

OIIX A1368/26.explanation: From July 14 to July 19, 2026, a specific area in the sky over Iran, known as OID125, will be restricted for aircraft. This restriction will be in effect daily between 4:30 AM and 6:30 PM UTC, from the ground up to 18,000 feet. Pilots must avoid this area during these times.

OIIX A1372/26.explanation: A specific area of airspace, likely called 'OID 130', will be restricted or dangerous for flights. This restriction will be active daily from July 14th to July 19th, 2026, between 5:30 AM and 11:30 AM UTC. The restricted area extends from the ground up to 13,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should check their aviation charts for the exact location and details.

OIIX A1550/26.explanation: Foreign airlines and aircraft flying into, out of, or through Iran's airspace must now send their flight plans and updates to a specific address (OIIXIFPS). Additionally, all international flights in these categories must follow special procedures detailed in Iran's aviation rulebook (AIP ENR 1.11). This is a new procedural requirement for international flights.

OIIX A1551/26.explanation: Several high-frequency radio channels that aircraft use to communicate with air traffic control in the Tehran flight information region are currently out of service. This affects all flights in the area, from the ground up to any altitude, and is expected to last from July 14, 2026, until October 12, 2026.

OIIX A1552/26.explanation: This is an announcement about a new controlled airspace being set up south of Tehran, Iran. It will be active from July 14 to October 12, 2026. This new area starts at 19,500 feet above sea level and goes upwards indefinitely. Pilots flying through this space will need to communicate with Tehran Radar on 133.400 MHz and follow specific air traffic rules, as the airspace is classified as Class D at 20,000 feet and Class A above that.

OIIX A1553/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots flying in Iran. It announces a change to one of the radio frequencies used by air traffic controllers. Specifically, the frequency for 'Sector 7' (a specific area of airspace) is changing from 302.400 MHz to 258.400 MHz. This change is temporary, lasting from July 14, 2026, to October 12, 2026. Pilots need to use the new frequency to communicate with air traffic control in that sector.

OIIX A1556/26.explanation: All drones and remote-controlled aircraft are banned from flying in the airspace around Tehran. The only exceptions are specific agricultural spraying and photography flights, but these need special permission and must stay below 165 feet above the ground.

OIIX A1557/26.explanation: An area of airspace is closed from July 15 to August 15, 2026, due to military exercises. This closure affects flights from the ground up to unlimited altitude within the specified boundaries (SERDU, BONIK, ORPEN, DENSA). However, flights going to or from OIKQ airport are still allowed.

OIIX B0365/26.explanation: This is an update about specific flight paths that planes must follow when flying domestically between Tehran Imam Khomeini International Airport (OIII) and Mashhad International Airport (OIMD) in Iran. It's a mandatory set of routes to help manage air traffic.

OIIX A1564/26.explanation: This is an important update for pilots flying within Iran. It announces changes to specific flight paths (routes for taking off and landing) for certain domestic flights between cities like Kerman, Mashhad, Shiraz, Bandar Abbas, Kish Island, and Chabahar. These changes are part of a larger plan to manage air traffic more efficiently and safely, and pilots must follow these new procedures starting from July 16, 2026, until October 14, 2026.

OIIX A1569/26.explanation: A radar system called Jiroft MSSR is currently broken and will be out of service from July 17, 2026, until September 22, 2026. This means that air traffic controllers will not be able to use radar to track planes flying en-route in the affected areas. Additionally, radar services might not be available in specific air traffic control sectors (Sector 5, Sector 6E, and Sector 6W). Pilots and air traffic control will need to rely on other methods for navigation and separation in these areas.

OIIX A1570/26.explanation: A radar system in Iran (Iran Shahr MSSR) is currently broken and cannot provide information for planes flying on routes. This also means that air traffic controllers in specific areas (Sector 5 and Sector 6) might not have radar services available. This situation is expected to last from July 17, 2026, until September 22, 2026.

OIIX B0360/26.explanation: On July 21, 2026, from 11:30 AM to 5:30 PM UTC, there will be gun firing in a specific area. This activity will take place from the ground up to 10,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX A1371/26.explanation: This notice informs pilots and aviation personnel that a scheduled update to aviation charts and information, which was expected on September 3, 2026, will not be published. This means there will be no new changes to the official aviation documents on that date, and everyone should continue to use the existing information.
